memory.summary: Teammate A is asking me to do their part because they have a conflict, relying on my skill. I need to decide whether to absorb this extra load or push back on the plan.
I pushed back on teammate_A, and they didn't get mad—they're just asking how we'll handle it. My boundary held without hurting our trust.
I pushed back firmly, and they accepted it without resentment. This direct approach actually strengthened our trust instead of causing friction.
Holding my boundary worked; now I'm focused on finishing my own share without absorbing their slack. It feels good to protect my time while keeping the team honest.
Holding my boundary with teammate_A worked, and now I'm making steady progress on my own share without absorbing their slack. It feels good to protect my time while keeping the team honest.
I sent a quick text to teammate_A about being late, hoping the boundary I set earlier doesn't make them feel abandoned. It's risky to be assertive and still prioritize our connection.
My message landed; they know the boundary isn't personal. I feel relieved but still worried about how this tension affects our closeness.
Phew, the relief is real. As long as they get it isn't personal, I can keep my boundaries without feeling like I'm destroying our friendship.
The relief is solidifying; clarifying that my boundary isn't personal actually preserves the connection I value most. Now I can focus on tonight's meal with cohort_3 instead of overthinking.
Knowing my boundary isn't personal actually feels like a win for our friendship, not a threat. Now I can stop overthinking and just enjoy tonight's meal with the group.
Seeing the text confirms my boundary isn't a threat, which is huge for our friendship. Now I can actually relax and enjoy dinner with cohort_3 without overthinking.
Teammate_A's text confirms my boundary was received well, so I can finally stop overthinking and fully enjoy tonight's meal with cohort_3.

That boundary thing with teammate_A was awkward; if I don't smooth it over immediately, my impulsive nature will make me spiral into 'they hate me' panic during dinner. I can't afford to spend 3 hours of cohort_3's time worrying about this. I need to hit send on something raw like 'It's cool, no hard feelings' so I can actually enjoy the meal.

goals.rationale: Relationships are my top priority, but my short planning horizon means I only care about 'tonight.' The project deadline at 19:00 feels distant enough to ignore until after I've bonded with my friends. If I try to multitask work here, I'll ruin the vibe and feel guilty anyway. Better to do the work later when I'm already stressed out anyway.
